cards.text: How does the countercurrent multiplier work?
cards.answer: Loop of Henle creates medullary osmotic gradient. Descending limb: permeable to water (water leaves), impermeable to solute. Ascending limb: impermeable to water, actively pumps NaCl out (Na-K-2Cl cotransporter). Creates gradient from 300 (cortex) to 1200 mOsm/L (inner medulla). Essential for concentrating urine.

tip.tip: The loop creates the gradient, the collecting duct uses it.

cards.answer: Low BP/Na+ -> JG cells release renin -> renin converts angiotensinogen (liver) to angiotensin I -> ACE (lungs) converts to angiotensin II -> vasoconstriction + aldosterone release (adrenal cortex) -> Na+ and water retention -> BP rises. ACE inhibitors block this pathway.

tip.tip: The kidney's blood pressure rescue system.

cards.text: How does the kidney regulate acid-base balance?
cards.answer: Reabsorbs filtered HCO3- (mainly PCT, via H+ secretion). Generates new HCO3- by excreting H+ as titratable acid (H2PO4-) and ammonium (NH4+). In acidosis: increases H+ secretion and NH4+ production. In alkalosis: reduces HCO3- reabsorption.

tip.tip: The kidney is the slow but powerful pH defender.

cards.text: What is clearance and how is GFR measured?
cards.answer: Clearance = volume of plasma completely cleared of a substance per unit time. C = (U x V) / P. Inulin clearance = GFR (gold standard, freely filtered, not reabsorbed or secreted). Creatinine clearance approximates GFR clinically (slightly overestimates due to secretion).

tip.tip: Inulin is the gold standard marker.

cards.answer: Autoregulatory mechanism: increased NaCl at macula densa (distal tubule) -> signals JG apparatus -> afferent arteriole constriction -> reduces GFR. Prevents excessive solute loss. Part of renal autoregulation that maintains constant GFR over BP range 80-180 mmHg.

Mineralocorticoid from zona glomerulosa. Acts on principal cells of collecting duct. Increases ENaC (Na+ channels) on luminal side -> Na+ reabsorption. Increases K+ and H+ secretion. Stimulated by angiotensin II and hyperkalaemia. Blocked by spironolactone.

cards.text: What is the renal threshold for glucose?
cards.answer: Plasma glucose level above which glucose appears in urine (glucosuria). Normal threshold: ~180 mg/dL. Below this, all filtered glucose is reabsorbed by SGLT-2 (PCT). In diabetes, plasma glucose exceeds threshold -> glucosuria -> osmotic diuresis -> polyuria.

tip.tip: Transport maximum is saturated above this level.

cards.text: What happens in acute kidney injury?
cards.answer: Rapid decline in GFR. Pre-renal (most common): reduced renal perfusion (dehydration, shock). Renal/intrinsic: tubular necrosis, glomerulonephritis. Post-renal: obstruction (stones, BPH). Features: oliguria, rising creatinine, hyperkalaemia, metabolic acidosis.

tip.tip: Pre-renal is the most common and most reversible.
